sections.title: Mystery Masala
sections.content_summary: The chapter opens at a school food festival where students encounter 'Mystery Masala', a stall serving Ugadi Pachadi — a traditional dish from Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, and Telangana. The dish introduces the six tastes: sweet (meetha), sour (khatta), salty (namkeen), pungent (teekha), bitter (karva), and astringent (kasaila). These six tastes are collectively known as Shad Rasa in Ayurveda, and balanced food containing all six tastes is considered good for health.

tasks.task_text: If you had to organise a food festival in your school, which dishes would you like to include? Make a list.
Can you think of at least five ingredients or food items that match each of the tastes listed below? Write them down in the spaces provided.
Discuss in small groups and identify the food items having at least three different tastes.

sections.title: A Stall of 'Super Food'
sections.content_summary: Students visit a millet foods stall where they learn about the nutritional richness of millets such as jowar, bajra, and jau. Traditional knowledge is highlighted through a student recalling that her grandmother ate jowar and bajra roti in winters and jau roti in summers — a seasonal food wisdom practice. Millets are presented as rich sources of nutrients, connecting traditional dietary practices with modern understanding of healthy food.

tasks.task_text: Find out more about a millet-based food prepared at your home.

sections.title: How Does Food Help Us?
sections.content_summary: This section explains why our bodies need a variety of food by introducing three food groups: energy-giving foods (rice, wheat, potato, millets, ghee), body-building foods (pulses, legumes, milk, eggs, meat, fish), and protective foods (fruits and vegetables like carrot, orange, spinach, amla). No single food contains all the nutrients the body needs, so eating a variety is essential. India's regional food thalis are highlighted as a cultural example of naturally diverse and balanced eating.

tasks.task_text: Why do we need to eat different kinds of food items?
What will happen if we eat food from a single group of foods everyday?
Find out a 'food thali' from your region, and list its food items.

sections.title: Say 'No' to Junk Food
sections.content_summary: Junk food is defined as food high in oil, salt, and sugar that is harmful when consumed regularly and in large quantities. Examples include soft drinks, chips, bread pakoda, burgers, pizza, and over-processed packaged food. The section contrasts junk food with fresh home-cooked food, encouraging students to make healthier choices and identify items they will avoid.

tasks.task_text: Can you name a few more junk food items?
Why do you think we should avoid eating junk food?
List the junk food items that you will avoid.

sections.title: A Balanced Food Plate
sections.content_summary: A balanced plate is described as one that includes the right quantities from all three food groups — with protective foods (fruits and vegetables) forming the largest portion, followed by body-building foods, and then energy-providing foods. This section introduces Activity 1, a Make a Food Diary activity where students record their meals over a week and reflect on healthy versus unhealthy food choices and missing food groups.

tasks.task_text: Make a Food Diary: Create a food diary of your daily routine for a week. Write down what you eat in a day for breakfast, lunch and dinner. Encircle the food items that are healthy and put an 'X' mark for items which are not healthy. Identify the food groups missing in your daily routine. Discuss with your parents how these items can be included in the daily routine.

sections.title: Water for Health
sections.content_summary: Water is presented as an essential component of diet alongside food. It aids digestion, keeps skin and body healthy, and removes waste through urine, sweat, and faeces. Eating water-rich fruits like watermelon and cucumber is suggested as an additional way to maintain body hydration. Drinking sufficient water is emphasised as a key healthy habit.

sections.title: Different Methods of Cooking
sections.content_summary: Students learn about different ways food is prepared — boiling, steaming, roasting, grilling, frying, and baking — through a Write task identifying which cooking method applies to common Indian food items. The section also notes that some foods like fruit chaat and vegetable salads can be eaten without cooking. Two informational facts are shared: the meaning of 'Chef' in French, and the use of solar cookers as an environment-friendly cooking method.

tasks.task_text: Identify different methods used to cook the food items given in the list below. One example is given for you. You can take help from your family members and add more items to the list.
Do you help in cooking at home?
Do you think all family members should know how to cook? Why?

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: The chapter closes with a reflective section containing four hands-on tasks. Students create a food collage classifying food items into the three food groups, check labels on packaged food at home, organise a school food festival with home-made food, and complete a word-search grid to identify food-related vocabulary.

tasks.task_text: Hands-on activity — food collage: Cut out pictures of food items and create a collage with three sections: energy-giving foods, body-building foods, and protective foods.

Check the label: At home, pick up any one packaged food item from your kitchen. Check the label and note down the expiry date, and also two main ingredients. Why is it important to check labels before buying or eating packaged food items?
Organise a food festival: Students may bring home-made food and organise a food festival at school. Parents may be encouraged to participate in the food festival.
Find and circle: Circle the words related to food in the grid below.

dual_strand.natural: Nutritional properties of food — energy-giving, body-building, and protective functions of different food groups
dual_strand.human_cultural: Traditional Indian food diversity — regional thalis, millets (jowar, bajra, jau), Ugadi Pachadi, Shad Rasa in Ayurveda; mindful eating, cooking practices, and reducing food waste
